units.text:
Arrays are the most fundamental data structure. Every other data structure either uses arrays internally or exists to address their limitations.
In this section,I'll teach you four techniques that transform how you think about array problems. Prefix sums let you answer range queries in O(1).
Difference arrays let you perform range updates in O(1). Two pointers reduce O(n2)searches to O(n). Sliding window solves subarray problems without nested loops. By the end of this section,you'll solve array problems in O(n)that others solve in O(n2).

You already know arrays: contiguous memory, O(1) access by index, O(n) search. I won't rehash basics. What you might not know is how to exploit array structure for speed.

The next technique I'll show you turns O(n) range queries into O(1). That's not a typo.

One million queries on a million elements goes from a trillion operations to just one million. How? By precomputing the right information.

Here's a problem you'll see constantly: given an array,answer many queries asking for the sum of elements from index l to r.
The naive approach loops from l to r and adds up elements. Each query takes O(n)time. With q queries,that's O(nq)total.
If n=100,000 and q=100,000,you're doing 10 billion operations. That's too slow. There's a better way.

A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums. For array arr,the prefix sum prefix[i] equals the sum of all elements from index 0 to i−1.
prefix[i]=arr[0]+arr[1]+⋯+arr[i−1] We set prefix[0]=0 by convention. So prefix has length n+1 when arr has length n. Why store cumulative sums?
Because any range sum you can compute from two prefix values. That's the core idea.

Here's why prefix sums work. The sum from index l to r equals: sum(l,r)=prefix[r+1]−prefix[l] Think about it: prefix[r+1] contains everything from 0 to r.
Subtracting prefix[l] removes everything from 0 to l−1. What's left is exactly the sum from l to r. Two array lookups and one subtraction. That's O(1)per query,no matter how big the range.

Trace through an example. Array: [2,4,1,3,5].
Building prefix sums:
prefix[0]=0
prefix[1]=0+2=2
prefix[2]=2+4=6
prefix[3]=6+1=7
prefix[4]=7+3=10
prefix[5]=10+5=15
Query: sum from index 1 to 3(elements 4,1,3).
Answer: prefix[4]−prefix[1]=10−2=8. Check: 4+1+3=8. It works.

Given an integer array nums,handle multiple queries of the form: what is the sum of elements between indices left and right inclusive?
You're solving the textbook prefix sum application. You'll build the prefix array once,then answer each query in constant time. Read the problem statement carefully.
Notice the constraints: up to 104 queries. Without prefix sums,that's 108 operations. With prefix sums,it's 104.

Building the prefix array takes one pass through the input:
Pseudocode


```
function buildPrefixSum(nums)n :=length of nums
    prefix :=array of size(n+1)prefix[0] :=0
    for i from 0 to n - 1
        prefix[i+1] :=prefix[i]+nums[i]
    return prefix

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).
Notice how each prefix value builds on the previous one. You're not recalculating sums from scratch each time.

Once you have the prefix array,queries are trivial: 
```pseudocode
function sumRange(left,right):
return prefix[right+1] - prefix[left]
Time:


```


```

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
class NumArray
    prefix :=empty array

    function init(nums)n :=length of nums
        prefix :=array of size(n+1)prefix[0] :=0
        for i from 0 to n - 1
            prefix[i+1] :=prefix[i]+nums[i]

    function sumRange(left,right)return prefix[right+1] - prefix[left]

```

Initialization: O(n)time,O(n)space. Each query: O(1)time.

What did you learn?
1. Precomputation trades space for query speed. You spend O(n)space to make queries O(1).
2. Cumulative sums let you compute any range sum by subtraction. Here's the core prefix sum trick.
3. The+1 offset in prefix arrays simplifies edge cases. prefix[0]=0 means you never special-case the start of the array. This pattern generalizes. Can you extend it to two dimensions?

What if you have a matrix instead of an array? Can you answer submatrix sum queries in O(1)? Yes. The same idea applies,but you need inclusion-exclusion to avoid double-counting. For a 2D prefix sum,prefix[i][j] stores the sum of all elements in the rectangle from(0,0)to(i−1,j−1).

A 2D prefix sum array stores cumulative sums over rectangles.
For matrix mat: prefix[i][j]=∑r=0i−1​∑c=0j−1​mat[r][c] You get the sum of all elements in the rectangle with top-left(0,0)and bottom-right(i−1,j−1).
Building this array takes O(nm)time for an n×m matrix. After that,any submatrix sum is O(1).

To query the sum of a rectangle from(r1​,c1​)to(r2​,c2​): sum=prefix[r2​+1][c2​+1]−prefix[r1​][c2​+1]−prefix[r2​+1][c1​]+prefix[r1​][c1​] Why four terms?
You start with the big rectangle,subtract two overlapping pieces,but that removes the top-left corner twice. Add it back once. You're using inclusion-exclusion.
Draw it on paper if it's not clear. The picture makes the formula obvious.

Building the 2D prefix array also uses inclusion-exclusion: prefix[i][j]=mat[i−1][j−1]+prefix[i−1][j]+prefix[i][j−1]−prefix[i−1][j−1] You add the current cell,plus the rectangle above,plus the rectangle to the left.
But the top-left rectangle gets counted twice,so subtract it once. Initialize prefix[0][j]=0 and prefix[i][0]=0 for all i,j. This handles edge cases cleanly.

Given a 2D matrix,handle multiple queries asking for the sum of elements inside a rectangle defined by its upper left corner(row1,col1)and lower right corner(row2,col2).
This extends the 1D prefix sum to two dimensions. The same trade-off applies: O(nm)preprocessing for O(1)queries. Read the problem. Notice there can be 104 queries on a 200×200 matrix.

Here's how to build the 2D prefix array:
Pseudocode


```
function build2DPrefix(matrix)rows :=number of rows in matrix
    cols :=number of cols in matrix
    prefix :=2D array of size(rows+1)x(cols+1),all zeros

    for i from 1 to rows
        for j from 1 to cols
            prefix[i][j] :=matrix[i-1][j-1]+prefix[i-1][j]+prefix[i][j-1]
                          - prefix[i-1][j-1]
    return prefix

```

Time: O(nm). Space: O(nm).

Querying uses the inclusion-exclusion formula:


```pseudocode
function sumRegion(row1, col1, row2, col2)
    return prefix[row2+1][col2+1]
         - prefix[row1][col2+1]
         - prefix[row2+1][col1]
         + prefix[row1][col1]

```

Time: O(1). Four array accesses, three arithmetic operations.

Trace this on a small example. Draw the four rectangles. The formula will make sense.

What did you learn?
1. Prefix sums generalize to higher dimensions. The pattern is the same: precompute cumulative values,answer queries by subtraction.
2. Inclusion-exclusion handles overlapping regions. When you add overlapping areas,subtract the intersection.
3. The+1 offset pattern persists. It keeps edge cases clean in any dimension. Next,I'll show you the inverse operation. What if you need to update ranges instead of query them?

New problem: you have an array,and you need to add a value v to all elements from index l to r. You'll do this many times,then read the final array.
Naive approach: loop from l to r,add v to each element. Each update takes O(n)time. Can you do better? Yes. Difference arrays let you perform range updates in O(1).

A difference array stores the difference between consecutive elements: diff[i]=arr[i]−arr[i−1] With diff[0]=arr[0].
Notice: if you take the prefix sum of the difference array,you get back the original array. Difference arrays and prefix sums are inverses of each other. This inverse relationship is what makes O(1)range updates possible.

To add v to all elements from index l to r,you only need to modify two positions in the difference array: diff[l]+=v diff[r+1]−=v That's it.
Two operations,regardless of how big the range is. Why does this work?
When you later compute the prefix sum of diff,adding v at position l propagates forward to all later positions. Subtracting v at r+1 cancels it for positions after r.

Array: [0,0,0,0,0]. You want to add 3 to indices 1 through 3. Difference array starts as: [0,0,0,0,0].
Update: diff[1]+=3,diff[4]−=3.
Now diff=[0,3,0,0,−3].
Take prefix sum of diff: [0,0+3,0+3+0,0+3+0+0,0+3+0+0−3]=[0,3,3,3,0].
Elements at indices 1,2,3 increased by 3. The rest stayed at 0.

You have an array of length n initialized to zeros. You're given k update operations. Each operation adds a value val to all elements from index start to end inclusive.
Return the final array. You're solving the textbook difference array problem. With k updates and length n,naive approach is O(kn). With difference arrays,it's O(k+n). Read the problem carefully.

The algorithm has two phases:
1. Process all updates on the difference array. Each update modifies exactly two positions.
2. Compute the prefix sum of the difference array to get the final result. This separates the O(k)update phase from the O(n)reconstruction phase. Total: O(k+n). Time: O(k+n). Space: O(n).

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function getModifiedArray(length,updates)diff :=array of size length,all zeros

    // Phase 1: Apply all updates to difference array
    for each(start,end,val)in updates
        diff[start]+=val
        if end+1 < length
            diff[end+1] -=val

    // Phase 2: Compute prefix sum to get final array
    for i from 1 to length - 1
        diff[i]+=diff[i - 1]

    return diff

```

Time: O(k+n). Space: O(n).

What did you learn?
1. Prefix sums and difference arrays are inverses. One undoes the other.
2. Prefix sums enable fast range queries. Difference arrays enable fast range updates.
3. Choose based on your workload. Many queries,few updates? Use prefix sums. Many updates,one final read? Use difference arrays. You now have two tools. Next,I'll show you a technique that doesn't require any preprocessing at all.

New problem: given a sorted array,find two numbers that sum to a target. Brute force: check all pairs. That's O(n2). There's a technique that solves this in O(n)with no extra space. It's called two pointers,and it's one of the most useful patterns in competitive programming.

Two pointers is a technique where you maintain two indices that move through the array based on some condition.
The classic setup: one pointer starts at the beginning,one at the end. They move toward each other until they meet or find the answer. Why does this work?
Because the array is sorted,moving a pointer in one direction has a predictable effect on the sum. You can eliminate possibilities without checking them.

With pointers left and right on a sorted array: If arr[left]+arr[right]<target: move left right. The sum is too small,and moving left increases it.
If arr[left]+arr[right]>target: move right left. The sum is too big,and moving right decreases it. If they're equal: you found the answer.
Each step eliminates at least one element from consideration. After at most n steps,you've checked all possibilities.

Given a sorted array of integers and a target sum,find two numbers such that they add up to the target. Return the indices(1-indexed). The array is already sorted.
The sorted property makes two pointers work. If it weren't sorted,you'd need a different approach(like a hash map,which I'll cover later). Read the problem. There's exactly one solution.

units.unit_name: Two Sum Sorted - Why It Works
units.text:
Why does two pointers find the answer? Suppose the answer is at indices i and j with i<j. Initially,left≤i and right≥j.
Every time you move left right,it's because the current sum is too small. Since the array is sorted,the correct left index must be further right.
So you never skip past i. Similarly,you never skip past j when moving right left. Eventually,left=i and right=j.

Two pointers start at opposite ends of the sorted array:
Pseudocode


```
function twoSum(numbers,target)left :=0
    right :=length of numbers - 1

    while left < right
        sum :=numbers[left]+numbers[right]
        if sum=target
            return [left+1,right+1]  // 1-indexed
        else if sum < target
            left :=left+1
        else
            right :=right - 1

    return []  // no solution found

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(1).

What did you learn?
1. Two pointers works on sorted arrays where you're searching for pairs satisfying some condition.
2. The trick is that moving one pointer has a predictable effect. You can eliminate candidates without checking them.
3. No extra space needed. You're just moving indices. Two pointers appears in many forms: finding pairs,removing duplicates,partitioning arrays. The pattern stays the same.

New problem type: find some property of contiguous subarrays. Maybe the maximum sum,or the longest with distinct elements,or the shortest with sum at least k.
Brute force: enumerate all subarrays. There are O(n2)of them,and processing each takes O(n). That's O(n3). Sliding window reduces this to O(n)for many problems.

For a fixed window of size k:
1. Build the initial window with the first k elements.
2. Slide the window: add the next element,remove the first element.
3. Update your answer at each position. Each element enters once and leaves once. Total: O(n). This pattern works for maximum sum of size k,average of size k,or any aggregate over fixed-size subarrays.

Given an integer array nums and an integer k,find the contiguous subarray of length k that has the maximum average value.
You're looking at the simplest fixed-window problem. You maintain the sum of the current window. When sliding,add the new element and subtract the old one. Read the problem. The constraints allow O(n)but not O(nk).

questions.title: Maximum Average Subarray I - Read Statement

units.text:
Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function findMaxAverage(nums,k)// Build initial window
    windowSum :=sum of nums[0] to nums[k-1]
    maxSum :=windowSum

    // Slide the window
    for i from k to length of nums - 1
        windowSum :=windowSum+nums[i] - nums[i - k]
        maxSum :=max(maxSum,windowSum)return maxSum / k

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(1).

questions.title: Maximum Average Subarray I - Implement Solution

units.text:
Variable-size windows grow and shrink based on a condition. The pattern is:
1. Expand: move right forward,adding the new element to the window.
2. Shrink: while the window violates some constraint,move left forward,removing elements.
3. Update: after each expansion,update your answer. Here's the trick: each element is added once and removed at most once. Even though there's a nested loop,total work is O(n).

Given a string,find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters. You're solving the classic variable-size sliding window problem.
You expand the window until you see a repeat,then shrink until the constraint is satisfied again. Read the problem. The string can have up to 5×104 characters.

You need to track which characters are in the current window. A hash set works well. When you add a character that's already in the set,the window has a repeat.
Shrink from the left until the repeat is gone. Why does this work? The window always contains a valid substring(no repeats). You're finding the longest such window.

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function lengthOfLongestSubstring(s)charSet :=empty set
    left :=0
    maxLength :=0

    for right from 0 to length of s - 1
        // Shrink window until no repeat
        while s[right] is in charSet
            remove s[left] from charSet
            left :=left+1

        // Expand window
        add s[right] to charSet
        maxLength :=max(maxLength,right - left+1)return maxLength

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(min(n,m))where m is the character set size.

What did you learn?
1. Sliding window works for contiguous subarray/substring problems where you need to find an optimal window satisfying some constraint.
2. Fixed windows slide one position at a time. Variable windows expand and shrink based on conditions.
3. Each element is processed at most twice(once added,once removed). You get O(n)time despite nested loops. Look for keywords: "contiguous," "subarray," "substring," "window of size k."

You now have four powerful array techniques:
1. Prefix sums: Precompute cumulative sums for O(1)range queries. Works in 1D and 2D.
2. Difference arrays: The inverse of prefix sums. Enables O(1)range updates.
3. Two pointers: Use sorted structure to find pairs in O(n)without extra space.
4. Sliding window: Solve subarray problems in O(n)by processing each element at most twice. These techniques combine with everything else you'll learn. Learn them,and half of competitive programming problems become tractable. In the next section,I'll show you stacks,a simple data structure with surprising power.

A stack is one of the simplest data structures. You add to the top,remove from the top. That's it.
But this simplicity enables clean solutions to problems involving nested structures,matching pairs,and finding relationships between elements.
In this section,I'll show you how. By the end,you'll solve problems like "largest rectangle in histogram" in O(n)using a technique called monotonic stacks.
Most people struggle with these problems. You won't.

A stack is a Last-In-First-Out(LIFO)data structure. Think of a stack of plates: you add plates to the top and remove plates from the top.
Three operations:

Push: add an element to the top
Pop: remove and return the top element
Peek(or top): view the top element without removing it

All three operations are O(1). You never access elements in the middle directly.

Trace some operations on an empty stack: Push 3: stack is [3]
Push 7: stack is [3,7]
Push 1: stack is [3,7,1]
Pop: returns 1,stack is [3,7]
Peek: returns 7,stack stays [3,7]
Pop: returns 7,stack is [3]
The rightmost element is the "top." The last element you added is the first one you remove.

In most languages,you implement a stack using a dynamic array:
Pseudocode


```
class Stack
    items :=empty array

    function push(x)append x to items

    function pop()return remove last element from items

    function peek()return items[length of items - 1]

    function isEmpty()return length of items=0

```

In practice,use the built-in: C++has  `stack`,Python has  `list`(use  `append`  and  `pop`),Java has  `Stack`  or  `Deque` .

Stacks appear whenever you need to remember something and then process it in reverse order:
1. Parentheses matching: push opens,pop on closes
2. Expression evaluation: handle operators and operands
3. Undo functionality: push actions,pop to undo
4. DFS traversal: iterative depth-first search
5. Call stack: how recursion works
Whenever you see "matching pairs" or "most recent unpaired element," think stack.

Consider the string "(()())". How do you verify the parentheses are balanced? You need to match each close with its corresponding open.
Here's the trick: each close matches the most recent unmatched open. "Most recent" means LIFO. That's a stack. Push each open parenthesis.
When you see a close,pop and check if it matches. If the stack is empty at the end and every close matched,the string is valid.

Given a string containing just the characters '(',')','{','}','[',and ']',determine if the input string is valid. A string is valid if:
1. Open brackets are closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets are closed in the correct order. You're looking at the entry-level stack problem. Every competitive programmer should be able to solve it in two minutes.

The algorithm is simple:
1. For each character in the string:

If it's an open bracket,push it onto the stack.
If it's a close bracket,check if the stack is non-empty and the top matches. If so,pop. Otherwise,return false.

2. After processing all characters,return true if the stack is empty. An empty stack at the end means every open found its matching close. Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

What did you learn?
1. Stacks naturally handle nested structures. Each open waits on the stack until its matching close arrives.
2. The "most recent" property is what makes stacks work for matching. Any close bracket must match the most recent open.
3. Edge cases matter: empty stack when you need to pop,non-empty stack at the end. This problem is simple,but the pattern generalizes to expression parsing,HTML tag matching,and more.

Parentheses matching was easy. Now consider this: for each element in an array,find the next element to the right that is greater.
Array: [2,1,2,4,3] Answers: [4,2,4,−1,−1] For element 2 at index 0,the next greater is 4 at index 3.
For element 4,there's nothing greater to its right,so the answer is−1. Brute force is O(n2). There's an O(n)solution using a special type of stack.

A monotonic stack maintains elements in sorted order. Either:
1. Monotonic decreasing: elements decrease from bottom to top
2. Monotonic increasing: elements increase from bottom to top
When you push a new element,you first pop all elements that violate the monotonic property. This "cleanup" before pushing is what gives monotonic stacks their power.

For "next greater element," use a monotonic decreasing stack(biggest on bottom).
When you pop an element because the current element is larger,you've found the answer for the popped element.
The current element is its "next greater." Elements that are never popped(they're bigger than everything after them)have no next greater. They stay on the stack until the end.
Each element is pushed once and popped at most once. Total: O(n).

Array: [2,1,2,4,3]. Process left to right,maintain decreasing stack. i=0,val=2: stack empty,push 2. Stack: [2]
i=1,val=1: 1<2,push 1.
Stack: [2,1]
i=2,val=2: 2>1,pop 1(answer for index 1 is 2),push 2. Stack: [2,2]
i=3,val=4: 4>2,pop both 2s(answers are 4),push 4.
Stack: [4]
i=4,val=3: 3<4,push 3. Stack: [4,3]
Remaining elements(4 and 3)have no next greater.

You have two arrays nums1 and nums2 where nums1 is a subset of nums2. For each element in nums1,find its next greater element in nums2. Return−1 if there is none.
This problem has a twist: you need to find next greater elements in nums2,then look up the answers for elements in nums1. Use a monotonic stack on nums2,store results in a hash map,then query for nums1 elements.

questions.title: Next Greater Element I - Read Statement

units.text:
Two phases:
1. Process nums2 with a monotonic decreasing stack. When you pop an element,store its next greater in a hash map.
2. For each element in nums1,look up its next greater in the hash map. Return−1 if not found. The hash map bridges the two arrays. You compute all next-greater relationships once,then answer queries in O(1). Time: O(n+m). Space: O(n).

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function nextGreaterElement(nums1,nums2)nextGreater :=empty hash map
    stack :=empty stack

    for each num in nums2
        while stack is not empty and num > top of stack
            popped :=pop from stack
            nextGreater[popped] :=num
        push num onto stack

    result :=array of size length of nums1
    for i from 0 to length of nums1 - 1
        if nums1[i] in nextGreater
            result[i] :=nextGreater[nums1[i]]
        else
            result[i] :=-1

    return result

```

Time: O(n+m). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Next Greater Element I - Implement Solution

units.text:
What did you learn?
1. Monotonic stacks find "next greater"(or "next smaller")relationships in O(n).
2. When you pop from a monotonic stack,you've found a relationship. The current element is the answer for the popped element.
3. Elements remaining on the stack have no answer. They're bigger than everything to their right. This pattern appears in many forms. You'll see another variation.

Given an array of daily temperatures,return an array where each element says how many days until a warmer temperature. If there is no future warmer day,put 0.
Example: [73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73] Answer: [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0] You're solving "next greater element" but you return distances instead of values. You'll need to store indices on the stack,not values.

Instead of storing values on the stack,store indices. When you pop index j because temperature at index i is higher: answer[j]=i−j You get the number of days between j and the next warmer day at i. The stack comparison uses temperatures[stack.top()],but you push and pop indices.

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function dailyTemperatures(temperatures)n :=length of temperatures
    answer :=array of size n,all zeros
    stack :=empty stack  // stores indices

    for i from 0 to n - 1
        while stack is not empty and temperatures[i] > temperatures[top of stack]
            j :=pop from stack
            answer[j] :=i - j
        push i onto stack

    return answer

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

What did you learn?
1. When you need distances instead of values,store indices on the stack.
2. The monotonic property still applies: compare values at indices,but push/pop indices.
3. Elements left on the stack(cooler than everything after)get 0 automatically because the array was initialized to zeros. You'll handle a harder problem that uses this pattern in a more subtle way.

Given an array of bar heights representing a histogram,find the area of the largest rectangle that can be formed.
Example: heights=[2,1,5,6,2,3] Answer: 10(rectangle of height 5 and width 2 using bars at indices 2 and 3)You're solving a classic interview problem.
Brute force is O(n2). The monotonic stack solution is O(n).

questions.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram - Read Statement

units.text:
For each bar,ask: how far can it extend left and right without encountering a shorter bar?
If bar at index i has height h,and it can extend from index l to r,its maximum rectangle has area h×(r−l+1). The answer is the maximum area over all bars.
Finding the extension limits for all bars in O(n)is the challenge. Monotonic stacks do exactly this.

Use a monotonic increasing stack(smallest on bottom). Store indices. When you encounter a bar shorter than the stack top,the top bar can't extend further right.
Pop it and calculate its area. The left boundary is the new stack top(or−1 if empty). The right boundary is the current index minus one.
After processing all bars,pop remaining elements. Their right boundary is n(they extend to the end).

Heights: [2,1,5,6,2,3]. Maintain increasing stack of indices. i=0,h=2: push 0. Stack: [0]
i=1,h=1: 1<2,pop 0. Area=2×(1−(−1)−1)=2.
Push 1. Stack: [1]
i=2,h=5: push 2. Stack: [1,2]
i=3,h=6: push 3. Stack: [1,2,3]
i=4,h=2: pop 3,area=6×1=6.
Pop 2,area=5×2=10. Push 4. Stack: [1,4]
i=5,h=3: push 5. Stack: [1,4,5]
Pop remaining: areas 3,8,6. Maximum: 10.

When you pop bar j because bar i is shorter:
1. Bar j can't extend right past index i−1(bar i is shorter).
2. Bar j can't extend left past the new stack top(that bar was shorter than j).
3. Between those boundaries,all bars are at least as tall as j(they were pushed after j and before being popped). So the width calculation(right−left−1)is correct.

What did you learn?
1. Monotonic stacks find "extension limits" for each element. How far can each bar extend before hitting something smaller?
2. The calculation happens at pop time,not push time. When you pop,you know both boundaries.
3. This problem combines next smaller on the right(why we pop)and previous smaller on the left(the new stack top). You've solved one of the hardest monotonic stack problems. If you understand it,you can handle most stack problems.

You've seen two types of monotonic stacks:
1. Monotonic decreasing(biggest on bottom): finds next greater element. Pop when current is larger.
2. Monotonic increasing(smallest on bottom): finds next smaller element. Pop when current is smaller. Choose based on what relationship you're looking for. "Next greater" uses decreasing. "Next smaller" uses increasing.

You can also find "previous greater" or "previous smaller" by processing right to left:
1. Next greater: process left to right,pop when current > top
2. Previous greater: process right to left,pop when current > top
3. Next smaller: process left to right,pop when current < top
4. Previous smaller: process right to left,pop when current < top
The histogram problem implicitly uses both next smaller(right boundary)and previous smaller(left boundary from stack).

Look for these signals:
1. "Matching pairs" or "balanced": parentheses,tags,brackets
2. "Next greater/smaller": direct monotonic stack
3. "How far can X extend": histogram pattern
4. "Most recent element satisfying condition": basic stack
5. "Process in reverse order": undo,backtracking
When you see these,think stack before trying other approaches.

Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1,compute how much water it can trap after raining.
Example: heights=[0,1,0,2,1,0,1,3,2,1,2,1] Answer: 6 This you can solve with stacks,but also with two pointers or dynamic programming. The stack approach uses the histogram insight: calculate trapped water layer by layer.

Use a monotonic decreasing stack. When you encounter a bar taller than the stack top:
1. Pop the top(the popped element forms a valley)2. If stack is now empty,no water can be trapped
3. Otherwise,calculate water between current bar,popped bar,and new top
The water at this layer has width(i−stack.top()−1)and height min(current,newTop)−popped. Accumulate water as you pop.

Here's the stack solution:
Pseudocode


```
function trap(height)stack :=empty stack  // stores indices
    water :=0

    for i from 0 to length of height - 1
        while stack is not empty and height[i] > height[top of stack]
            mid :=pop from stack
            if stack is empty
                break  // no left boundary
            left :=top of stack
            width :=i - left - 1
            h :=min(height[i],height[left])- height[mid]
            water :=water+width×h
        push i onto stack

    return water

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

You now understand stacks at a deep level:
1. Basic stacks: LIFO structure for matching,nesting,and reversal problems. O(1)operations.
2. Monotonic decreasing stacks: find next/previous greater element in O(n).
3. Monotonic increasing stacks: find next/previous smaller element in O(n).
4. The histogram pattern: when popping,you know both left and right boundaries. Stacks are simple but effective. The monotonic variant is one of the most useful techniques in competitive programming. Practice until the pattern becomes automatic. In the next section,I'll show you queues and their monotonic variant for sliding window problems.

A queue processes elements in the order they arrive. First in,first out. Think of a line at a coffee shop.
In this section,you'll learn queues,then their more powerful cousin: the deque(double-ended queue).
The highlight is the monotonic deque,which solves "sliding window maximum" in O(n). By the end,you'll handle problems that require efficient access to both the maximum and minimum over a sliding range.

A queue is a First-In-First-Out(FIFO)data structure. Elements leave in the same order they arrived.
Three operations:

Enqueue(or push): add an element to the back
Dequeue(or pop): remove and return the front element
Front(or peek): view the front element without removing

All three operations are O(1). Unlike stacks,you remove from the opposite end from where you add.

Trace operations on an empty queue: Enqueue 3: queue is [3]
Enqueue 7: queue is [3,7]
Enqueue 1: queue is [3,7,1]
Dequeue: returns 3,queue is [7,1]
Front: returns 7,queue stays [7,1]
Dequeue: returns 7,queue is [1]
Elements leave in the order 3,7,1,the same order they arrived. That's FIFO.

You can implement queues with:
1. Linked list: O(1)enqueue and dequeue,but more memory overhead.
2. Circular array: O(1)amortized,more cache-friendly. In practice,use the built-in: C++has  `queue`,Python has  `collections.deque`(use  `append`  and  `popleft`),Java has  `Queue`  interface with  `LinkedList` . Don't implement from scratch in contests. Use the standard library. Time: O(1)per operation. Space: O(n).

Queues appear when processing order matters:
1. BFS traversal: explore nodes level by level
2. Task scheduling: process jobs in arrival order
3. Buffering: handle data streams
4. Simulation: model real-world queues
The most important application for competitive programming is BFS. When you need shortest path in an unweighted graph,BFS with a queue is the answer.

Design a class that counts the number of recent requests within a certain time frame.
Implement a  `ping(t)`  method that adds a new request at time t and returns the number of requests in the past 3000 milliseconds(from t−3000 to t).
You're solving a warm-up queue problem. You need to track recent requests and remove old ones.

questions.title: Number of Recent Calls - Read Statement

units.text:
Use a queue to store timestamps of requests:
1. When  `ping(t)`  is called,enqueue t.
2. Dequeue all timestamps less than t−3000(they're too old).
3. Return the queue size. The queue always contains exactly the requests in the valid time window. Older requests get removed as newer ones arrive. Amortized time: O(1)per call. Space: O(n).

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
class RecentCounter
    queue :=empty queue

    function ping(t)enqueue t

        // Remove timestamps outside the window
        while front of queue < t - 3000
            dequeue

        return size of queue

```

Each timestamp is enqueued once and dequeued at most once. If there are n calls to  `ping`,total time is O(n). Amortized O(1)per call.

questions.title: Number of Recent Calls - Implement Solution

units.text:
What did you learn?
1. Queues naturally maintain a "sliding window" of elements. New elements enter at the back,old ones leave from the front.
2. Amortized analysis: even though the while loop can run multiple times per call,each element is enqueued and dequeued exactly once across all calls.
3. This pattern appears whenever you need to track "recent" items within some constraint. But queues have a limitation: you can only access the front. What if you need both ends?

Queues only let you add at the back and remove from the front. Sometimes you need more flexibility.
Consider the sliding window maximum problem: find the maximum in each window of size k as it slides through an array.
With a basic queue,you can't efficiently track the maximum. You need access to both ends,and you need to remove elements from either end. Enter the deque.

A deque(pronounced "deck")is a double-ended queue.
You can add and remove from both ends:

 `push_front` : add to front
 `push_back` : add to back
 `pop_front` : remove from front
 `pop_back` : remove from back
 `front` : view front
 `back` : view back

All operations are O(1). A deque combines the capabilities of both stacks and queues.

Most languages have built-in deques:

C++:  `deque<T>`  with  `push_front`,`push_back`,`pop_front`,`pop_back` 
Python:  `collections.deque`  with  `appendleft`,`append`,`popleft`,`pop` 
Java:  `ArrayDeque`  or  `LinkedList`  implementing  `Deque`  interface

Never implement a deque from scratch in contests.
Use the standard library. Focus on the algorithm. Time: O(1). Space: O(n).

Given an array nums and a window size k,return an array containing the maximum of each sliding window.
Example: nums=[1,3,−1,−3,5,3,6,7],k=3 Windows: [1,3,−1],[3,−1,−3],[−1,−3,5],[−3,5,3],[5,3,6],[3,6,7] Maxes: [3,3,5,5,6,7] Naive approach: for each window,scan all k elements to find the max.
Time: O(nk). With a monotonic deque,you can do this in O(n).

A monotonic deque maintains elements in sorted order,either increasing or decreasing from front to back. For sliding window maximum,use a monotonic decreasing deque.
The front always holds the maximum of the current window. When adding a new element,remove smaller elements from the back. They can never be the maximum while the larger element is in the window.

For sliding window maximum:
1. Store indices(not values)in the deque,so you can check if elements are in the current window.
2. Maintain decreasing order:  `nums[deque.front()] >=nums[deque.back()]` .
3. When adding index i,remove indices from the back while  `nums[i] >=nums[deque.back()]` . Smaller elements can't be the maximum while i is in the window.
4. Remove indices from the front that are outside the window(index <i−k+1).
5. The front is always the maximum of the current window.

Array: [1,3,−1,−3,5],k=3. Deque stores indices. i=0,val=1: deque empty,push 0. Deque: [0]
i=1,val=3: 3>1,pop 0,push 1.
Deque: [1]
i=2,val=−1:−1<3,push 2. Deque: [1,2]. Window complete. Max=nums[1]=3.
i=3,val=−3:−3<−1,push 3. Deque: [1,2,3].
Front 1 still in window. Max=3.
i=4,val=5: 5>−3,−1,3,pop all,push 4. Front 1 outside window anyway. Deque: [4].
Max=5. The front always gives the maximum.

Given an integer array nums and an integer k,return the maximum element in each sliding window of size k. You're solving the textbook monotonic deque problem.
The solution is O(n)because each element is added to and removed from the deque at most once. Read the problem. Constraints are up to 105 elements.

For each index i:
1. Remove indices from front that are outside window(<i−k+1).
2. Remove indices from back while nums[i]>=nums[deque.back()]. These can never be the answer.
3. Push i to back.
4. If i>=k−1(window is full),the front is the maximum. Each index is pushed once and popped at most once. Total: O(n). Time: O(n). Space: O(k).

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function maxSlidingWindow(nums,k)n :=length of nums
    deque :=empty deque // stores indices
    result :=empty array

    for i from 0 to n - 1
        // Remove indices outside current window
        while deque is not empty and front of deque < i - k+1
            pop front from deque

        // Maintain decreasing order
        while deque is not empty and nums[i] >=nums[back of deque]
            pop back from deque

        push i to back of deque

        // Window is full,record maximum
        if i >=k - 1
            append nums[front of deque] to result

    return result

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(k).

What did you learn?
1. Monotonic deques find extrema(max or min)over sliding windows in O(n).
2. Store indices,not values. You need indices to check if elements are in the current window.
3. Two maintenance operations: remove old elements from front(outside window),remove dominated elements from back(can't be the answer).
4. The front always holds the answer for the current window. This pattern applies to any "sliding window extremum" problem.

The mirror of sliding window maximum is sliding window minimum. Instead of a monotonic decreasing deque,use a monotonic increasing deque. The front holds the minimum.
Remove elements from back when the new element is smaller(not larger). Everything else stays the same. Some problems need both max and min simultaneously. Use two deques.

Given an integer array nums and an integer k,find the length of the shortest non-empty subarray with sum at least k. Return−1 if no such subarray exists.
This problem combines prefix sums with monotonic deques. It's harder than sliding window maximum. Arrays can contain negative numbers,which is why a simple sliding window doesn't work.

questions.title: Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K - Read Statement

units.text:
The sum of subarray from i+1 to j is prefix[j]−prefix[i]. You want this to be at least k.
For each j,you're looking for the largest i<j such that prefix[j]−prefix[i]≥k,which means prefix[i]≤prefix[j]−k.
If prefix[i1​]≥prefix[i2​] where i1​<i2​,then i1​ is never useful. Any j that could use i1​ could use i2​ instead and get a shorter subarray. This "dominated" insight means you can use a monotonic deque.

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function shortestSubarray(nums,k)n :=length of nums
    prefix :=array of size(n+1)prefix[0] :=0
    for i from 0 to n - 1
        prefix[i+1] :=prefix[i]+nums[i]

    deque :=empty deque // stores indices into prefix
    minLength :=infinity

    for j from 0 to n
        // Check valid subarrays from front
        while deque is not empty and prefix[j] - prefix[front of deque] >=k
            minLength :=min(minLength,j - front of deque)pop front from deque

        // Maintain increasing order
        while deque is not empty and prefix[j] <=prefix[back of deque]
            pop back from deque

        push j to back of deque

    if minLength=infinity
        return -1
    return minLength

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K - Implement Solution

units.text:
What did you learn?
1. Prefix sums turn subarray sum queries into two-point differences.
2. Monotonic deques can optimize searches over prefix values,not just array values.
3. The front removal differs from sliding window max: once an index gives a valid answer,it's consumed and removed.
4. Negative numbers complicate things. Without them,a simple two-pointer approach would work. You've solved a hard problem. If you understand it,you have a solid grasp of monotonic deques.

You can simulate a deque using two stacks. One stack for the front,one for the back. When one is empty and you need to access it,move elements from the other.
You get amortized O(1)per operation. It's rarely needed in contests,but useful if the language doesn't have a built-in deque.
Stacks and queues are fundamentally related. Deques unify both.

Look for these signals:
1. "Sliding window maximum/minimum": monotonic deque
2. "Shortest subarray with property X": often deque+prefix sums
3. "First/last element access needed": deque over queue
4. "Process from both ends": deque naturally If you only need one end,use a stack or queue. Deques are more powerful but slightly slower due to more general implementation.

Given an integer array nums and an integer k,return the maximum sum of a non-empty subsequence such that for every two consecutive integers in the subsequence,nums[i] and nums[j],where i<j,the condition j−i≤k is satisfied.
You're solving a dynamic programming problem where the transition looks at the last k states. A naive DP is O(nk). With a monotonic deque,it's O(n).

Let dp[i] be the maximum sum of a subsequence ending at index i.
Transition: dp[i]=nums[i]+max(0,maxj∈[i−k,i−1]​dp[j])You either start fresh at i(just nums[i])or extend from some dp[j] within distance k.
Naive implementation: O(nk)because you scan k previous states. With monotonic deque: O(n)by maintaining max over the sliding window of dp values.

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function constrainedSubsetSum(nums,k)n :=length of nums
    dp :=copy of nums
    deque :=empty deque // stores indices

    for i from 0 to n - 1
        // Get max from valid range
        if deque is not empty
            dp[i] :=max(dp[i],dp[i]+dp[front of deque])// Maintain decreasing order by dp value
        while deque is not empty and dp[i] >=dp[back of deque]
            pop back from deque

        push i to back of deque

        // Remove out-of-range indices
        if front of deque <=i - k
            pop front from deque

    return max element in dp

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

What did you learn?
1. When DP transitions look at the last k states,monotonic deques can optimize from O(nk)to O(n).
2. You've learned a general pattern. Any DP where you need max or min over a sliding window of previous states can use this technique.
3. The deque stores indices into the DP array,ordered by DP values. This optimization appears in advanced DP problems. Recognize the pattern: "max/min over previous k states."

You now understand queues and deques:
1. Queues: FIFO structure. needed for BFS and processing in arrival order.
2. Deques: Double-ended access. Add and remove from both ends in O(1).
3. Monotonic deques: Find max/min over sliding windows in O(n). Store indices,maintain sorted order,remove dominated elements.
4. DP optimization: When transitions look at max/min over last k states,use monotonic deques to go from O(nk)to O(n). The monotonic deque is less famous than the monotonic stack,but equally powerful. Learn both. In the next section,I'll show you hash tables for O(1)lookups.

What if you could check "have I seen this element before?" in constant time? That's what hash tables give you.
In this section,you'll learn hash sets and hash maps,then learn the patterns that make them powerful: frequency counting,two-sum,and prefix sum combinations. By the end,you'll recognize when a problem that looks O(n2)you can solve in O(n)with the right data structure.

A hash table(or hash map)stores key-value pairs with average O(1)operations:

Insert: add a key-value pair
Lookup: get the value for a key
Delete: remove a key-value pair
Contains: check if a key exists

A hash set is a simplified version that only stores keys(no values). It answers "is this element present?" in O(1).

A hash function converts a key to an array index. For key k: index=hash(k)modtableSize Good hash functions distribute keys uniformly.
Different keys mapping to the same index is called a collision. You don't need to implement hash functions in contests. Use the built-in: C++has  `unordered_map`  and  `unordered_set`,Python has  `dict`  and  `set` .

Average case: O(1)for insert,lookup,delete. Worst case: O(n)
if all keys collide(hash to the same index).
Collisions are rare with good hash functions and proper table sizing. In competitive programming,assume O(1)unless you're dealing with adversarial inputs.
Some judges craft test cases to break weak hash functions. If you get TLE,consider using a tree-based map(O(logn))instead. Time: O(1). Space: O(n).

Hash Set: stores unique elements. Use when you only care about presence.

"Have I seen this number?"
"Is this string in the dictionary?"

Hash Map: stores key-value pairs.
Use when you need to associate data with keys.

"How many times does each number appear?"
"What index did I first see this number at?"

In Python,`set`  is a hash set,`dict`  is a hash map. In C++,`unordered_set`  and  `unordered_map` .

The most common hash map pattern: count how many times each element appears.
Pseudocode


```
freq :=empty hash map
for each element in array
    if element in freq
        freq[element] :=freq[element]+1
    else
        freq[element] :=1

```

After this,`freq[x]`  tells you how many times  `x`  appeared. Time: O(n). Space: O(k)where k is the number of distinct elements.

Given two strings s and t,return true if t is an anagram of s,and false otherwise. An anagram uses the same characters with the same frequencies,just rearranged.
For example,"listen" and "silent" are anagrams. You're applying a direct application of frequency counting. Compare the frequency maps of both strings.

Two ways to solve this:
1. Build frequency maps for both strings,compare them. Time: O(n),Space: O(k)where k is alphabet size.
2. Build one frequency map for s,then decrement for each character in t. If any count goes negative or non-zero counts remain,not an anagram. Both work. The second uses less space if you're careful.

Here's the solution using one map:
Pseudocode


```
function isAnagram(s,t)if length of s !=length of t
        return false

    freq :=empty hash map

    for each char in s
        freq[char] :=freq[char]+1  // default 0

    for each char in t
        if char not in freq or freq[char]=0
            return false
        freq[char] :=freq[char] - 1

    return true

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(k)where k is the alphabet size.

What did you learn?
1. Frequency maps turn "same elements,different order" problems into "same counts" problems.
2. Early termination(length check)saves time on obvious non-matches.
3. For fixed alphabets(like lowercase English),an array of size 26 is faster than a hash map.
But hash maps work for any character set. This pattern extends to "permutation in string," "find all anagrams," and similar problems.

In the Arrays section,you solved two-sum on a sorted array using two pointers. But what if the array isn't sorted?
You could sort it first(O(nlogn)),but that changes indices. What if you need the original indices? Hash maps solve this in O(n)without sorting. The idea: for each element x,check if target−x exists.

For two-sum,you're looking for pairs(x,y)where x+y=target. So y=target−x. For each element x:
1. Check if target−x is in the hash map
2. If yes,you found a pair
3. Add x to the hash map
The hash map stores elements you've seen. For each new element,you check if its "complement" was seen before.

Given an array of integers nums and an integer target,return indices of the two numbers that add up to target.
Each input has exactly one solution,and you can't use the same element twice. You're implementing the most famous hash map problem. It's LeetCode problem #1 for a reason. Everyone should be able to solve it in under five minutes.

Use a hash map from value to index:
1. For each index i with value nums[i]:

Compute complement=target−nums[i]
If complement is in the map,return [map[complement],i]
Otherwise,add nums[i]→i to the map

2. If no pair found,return empty(but problem guarantees a solution exists). One pass through the array. Each element is checked and added once. Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function twoSum(nums,target)indexMap :=empty hash map  // value -> index

    for i from 0 to length of nums - 1
        complement :=target - nums[i]

        if complement in indexMap
            return [indexMap[complement],i]

        indexMap[nums[i]] :=i

    return []  // no solution(won't happen per problem)
```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

What did you learn?
1. The "complement lookup" pattern: instead of checking all pairs,for each element check if its complement exists.
2. Add to map after checking. This prevents using the same element twice.
3. Store value-to-index because you need to return indices,not values. This pattern generalizes: three-sum uses two-sum as a subroutine,four-sum uses three-sum,and so on.

Given an array of strings,group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order. Example:  `["eat","tea","tan","ate","nat","bat"]`  Output:  `[["bat"],["nat","tan"],["ate","eat","tea"]]`  The challenge: how do you create a hash map key that identifies all anagrams of a word?

Two approaches for the key:
1. Sorted string: sort the characters. "eat","tea","ate" all become "aet".
2. Character count: represent as a tuple of 26 counts. "eat" becomes(1,0,0,0,1,0,...,1,0,0). Sorted string is simpler to implement. Character count is faster for long strings(O(n)vs O(nlogn)). Both create a canonical form that's identical for all anagrams.

Here's the solution using sorted keys:
Pseudocode


```
function groupAnagrams(strs)groups :=empty hash map  // sorted string -> list of strings

    for each str in strs
        key :=sort characters of str
        if key not in groups
            groups[key] :=empty list
        append str to groups[key]

    return all values in groups

```

Time: O(n⋅klogk)where n is the number of strings and k is the maximum string length. Space: O(nk).

What did you learn?
1. Hash map keys can be complex: strings,tuples,or any hashable type.
2. The key should be a "canonical form" that's identical for equivalent items.
3. Different canonical forms have different trade-offs. Choose based on your constraints. This pattern appears in: grouping equivalent items,finding duplicates with custom equality,and caching with complex keys.

You learned prefix sums for range queries. You learned hash maps for fast lookup. What happens when you combine them? Problem: count subarrays with sum equal to k.
Brute force: check all O(n2)subarrays. With prefix sums,each check is O(1),but still O(n2)total. With hash maps,you can do this in O(n).
The trick: for each prefix sum,count how many previous prefix sums would give a valid subarray. Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

If prefix[j]−prefix[i]=k,then the subarray from i+1 to j has sum k. Rearranging: prefix[i]=prefix[j]−k.
For each j,count how many i<j have prefix[i]=prefix[j]−k. That's how many subarrays ending at j have sum k.
Use a hash map to count occurrences of each prefix sum. For each new prefix sum,look up how many times(current−k)has appeared.

Given an integer array nums and an integer k,return the total number of subarrays whose sum equals k. You're solving the textbook prefix sum+hash map problem.
The combination gives O(n)time. Note: the array can contain negative numbers,so the simple sliding window approach doesn't work.

questions.title: Subarray Sum Equals K - Read Statement

units.text:
Why start with freq[0]=1? Consider array [3] with k=3.
After processing:

prefixSum=3
We look for prefixSum−k=0 in the map
If 0 wasn't in the map,we'd miss this valid subarray

The prefix sum 0 represents the empty prefix(sum before index 0). A subarray from index 0 to j has sum prefix[j]−prefix[−1]=prefix[j]−0=prefix[j].

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function subarraySum(nums,k)prefixSum :=0
    count :=0
    freq :={0: 1}  // hash map with initial entry

    for each num in nums
        prefixSum :=prefixSum+num

        // Count subarrays ending here with sum k
        target :=prefixSum - k
        if target in freq
            count :=count+freq[target]

        // Update frequency map
        if prefixSum in freq
            freq[prefixSum] :=freq[prefixSum]+1
        else
            freq[prefixSum] :=1

    return count

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Subarray Sum Equals K - Implement Solution

units.text:
What did you learn?
1. Prefix sum+hash map is a powerful combination for subarray problems with exact sum constraints.
2. Initialize the hash map with {0:1} to handle subarrays starting at index 0.
3. This pattern works because you're doing "complement lookup" on prefix sums,just like two-sum on values. This technique solves many variations: subarrays divisible by k,subarrays with equal 0s and 1s,and more.

Given an unsorted array of integers,find the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence.
Example: [100,4,200,1,3,2] Output: 4(the sequence is [1,2,3,4])The constraint: you must solve this in O(n)time. Sorting would be O(nlogn).
The trick here is subtle. Hash sets give O(1)lookup,but how do you efficiently find consecutive sequences?

The trick: only start counting from the beginning of a sequence. A number x is the start of a sequence if x−1 is not in the set.
If x−1 exists,then x is in the middle of some sequence,and you'll count it when you start from the actual beginning.
This guarantees each element is visited at most twice(once in the set check,once in the counting loop). Total: O(n).

Phase 1: Add all numbers to a hash set.
Phase 2: For each number x in the set:

If x−1 is not in the set,x is a sequence start
Count consecutive numbers: x,x+1,x+2,... until not found
Update the maximum length

Why is this O(n)?
Each number is the start of exactly one sequence. The inner counting loop visits each number at most once across all iterations. Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function longestConsecutive(nums)numSet :=set of all elements in nums
    maxLength :=0

    for each num in numSet
        // Only start from sequence beginnings
        if(num - 1)not in numSet
            currentNum :=num
            currentLength :=1

            // Count consecutive numbers
            while(currentNum+1)in numSet
                currentNum :=currentNum+1
                currentLength :=currentLength+1

            maxLength :=max(maxLength,currentLength)return maxLength

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

What did you learn?
1. Hash sets turn O(n)searches into O(1)checks.
2. Clever iteration order avoids double-counting. Only start from sequence beginnings.
3. Amortized analysis: the nested loop looks O(n2),but each element is processed at most twice. Total is O(n). This problem shows that O(n)solutions sometimes require creative thinking,not just applying a pattern.

In competitive programming,some judges create adversarial test cases that cause many hash collisions,degrading O(1)to O(n). Signs of a hash attack: TLE on  `unordered_map`  but AC on  `map` . Solutions:
1. Use tree-based structures(`map`  in C++,`TreeMap`  in Java). O(logn)is slower but guaranteed.
2. Use a custom hash function that's harder to attack.
3. In Python,`dict`  is generally resistant but not immune.

Look for these signals:
1. "Find two elements with property X": complement lookup(two-sum pattern)2. "Count occurrences" or "frequency": frequency counting
3. "Group by equivalence": custom keys(group anagrams)4. "Subarray with sum/property": prefix sum+hash map
5. "Check if seen before": hash set
6. "Fast lookup needed": hash map
When you see O(n2)brute force and ask "can I do better?",hash tables are often the answer.

You now have hash table proficiency:
1. Hash sets and maps: O(1)average lookup,insert,delete.
2. Frequency counting: count occurrences of each element in O(n).
3. Complement lookup(two-sum): find pairs by checking if the complement exists.
4. Custom keys: group equivalent items using canonical forms.
5. Prefix sum+hash: count subarrays with exact sum in O(n).
6. Creative iteration: longest consecutive sequence shows that clever ordering avoids redundant work. Hash tables are your default when you need fast lookups. Learn these patterns and many O(n2)problems become O(n). In the next section,I'll show you heaps for efficient min/max tracking.

What if you need to repeatedly find and remove the smallest element from a collection? An unsorted array gives O(n)per removal.
Keep the array sorted in reverse and the minimum sits at the end: O(1)removal,but O(n)insertion. Heaps balance both: O(logn)insertion and removal,O(1)peek at the minimum.
In this section,you'll learn heaps,then learn patterns like k-th element finding,merging sorted lists,and the two-heaps technique for running medians.

A heap is a complete binary tree satisfying the heap property: Min-heap: every parent is smaller than or equal to its children. The root is the minimum.
Max-heap: every parent is greater than or equal to its children.
The root is the maximum. "Complete" means all levels are fully filled except possibly the last,which is filled left to right. This structure allows efficient array storage.

Min-heap with elements [1,3,2,7,6,4,5]: 
```pseudocode
1
/ 
3   2
/ \ / 
7  6 4  5
The


```
Note: siblings have no ordering relationship. $3$ and $2$ are unordered relative to each other.

```

Heaps are stored as arrays,not as linked nodes.
For element at index i:

Parent: ⌊(i−1)/2⌋
Left child: 2i+1
Right child: 2i+2

The heap [1,3,2,7,6,4,5] is stored exactly as written.
Index 0 is the root,indices 1 and 2 are its children,and so on. This compact representation has excellent cache performance and no pointer overhead.

To insert an element:
1. Add it at the end of the array(next position in the complete tree).
2. "Bubble up": while it's smaller than its parent(in a min-heap),swap with parent.
3. Stop when heap property is restored.
Pseudocode


```
function insert(heap,value)append value to heap
    i :=length of heap - 1
    while i > 0 and heap[i] < heap[parent(i)]
        swap heap[i] and heap[parent(i)]
        i :=parent(i)
```

At most O(logn)swaps,one per level.

To extract the minimum(in a min-heap):
1. Save the root value.
2. Move the last element to the root.
3. "Bubble down": while larger than a child,swap with the smaller child.
4. Return the saved value.
Pseudocode


```
function extractMin(heap)minVal :=heap[0]
    heap[0] :=last element of heap
    remove last element
    bubbleDown(heap,0)
return minVal

```

Bubbling down takes at most O(logn)swaps.

When bubbling down,always swap with the smaller child(in a min-heap):
Pseudocode


```
function bubbleDown(heap,i)n :=length of heap
    while true
        smallest :=i
        left :=2 * i+1
        right :=2 * i+2

        if left < n and heap[left] < heap[smallest]
            smallest :=left
        if right < n and heap[right] < heap[smallest]
            smallest :=right

        if smallest=i
            break
        swap heap[i] and heap[smallest]
        i :=smallest

```

Swapping with the smaller child ensures the heap property is maintained.

A priority queue is an abstract data type that supports:

Insert an element with a priority
Remove and return the highest priority element

Heaps are the standard implementation. "Highest priority" can mean smallest(min-heap)or largest(max-heap). In C++:  `priority_queue`(max-heap by default)In Python:  `heapq`  module(min-heap)In Java:  `PriorityQueue`(min-heap by default)

In contests,use the built-in heap: C++(max-heap by default):  `priority_queue<int> pq;`  For min-heap:  `priority_queue<int,vector<int>,greater<int>> pq;`  Python(min-heap):  `heapq.heappush(heap,val)`   `heapq.heappop(heap)`  For max-heap: negate values or use tuples with negative priority.
Don't implement heaps from scratch in contests. Use the standard library.

You have a collection of stones,each with a positive weight. Each turn,you take the two heaviest stones and smash them. If they're equal,both are destroyed.
Otherwise,the lighter one is destroyed and the heavier one loses weight equal to the lighter. Return the weight of the last stone,or 0 if no stones remain.
You're solving a warm-up heap problem. You repeatedly need the two largest elements.

Use a max-heap to always get the two heaviest stones:
1. Insert all stones into a max-heap.
2. While heap has more than one stone:

Extract the two largest: y and x(with y≥x)If y>x,insert y−x back into the heap

3. Return the remaining stone's weight,or 0 if empty. Each smash involves at most two extractions and one insertion: O(logn)per turn,at most n turns. Time: O(nlogn). Space: O(n).

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function lastStoneWeight(stones)heap :=max-heap containing all stones

    while size of heap > 1
        y :=extract max from heap
        x :=extract max from heap
        if y > x
            insert(y - x)into heap

    if heap is empty
        return 0
    return extract max from heap

```

Time: O(nlogn). Space: O(n).

What did you learn?
1. When you repeatedly need the maximum(or minimum),use a heap.
2. Simulation problems often need heaps when the order of operations depends on current state.
3. Max-heap vs min-heap: choose based on whether you need the largest or smallest. This was simple. You'll handle a more interesting pattern: finding the k-th largest element.

Given an array,find the k-th largest(or smallest)element. Approaches:
1. Sort: O(nlogn)time,O(1)extra space.
2. Min-heap of size k: O(nlogk)time,O(k)space.
3. Quickselect: O(n)average,O(n2)worst. The heap approach is effective and reliable. For k-th largest,maintain a min-heap of the k largest elements seen so far.

Why use a min-heap for k-th largest? It seems backwards. The trick: keep exactly k elements in a min-heap.
The heap root is the smallest of these k elements,which is the k-th largest overall. For each new element:

If smaller than heap root,ignore it(not in top k)If larger,remove the root and insert the new element

After processing all elements,the root is the k-th largest.

Given an integer array nums and an integer k,return the k-th largest element. Note: it's the k-th largest in sorted order,not the k-th distinct element. Example: nums=[3,2,1,5,6,4],k=2 Output: 5(sorted: [6,5,4,3,2,1],2nd largest is 5)You're solving the textbook heap problem for k-th element selection.

Maintain a min-heap of size k:
1. Insert the first k elements into the heap.
2. For each remaining element:

If it's larger than the heap root,extract root and insert element.
Otherwise,skip it.

3. Return the heap root. The heap always contains the k largest elements seen so far. Its root(minimum of these k)is the k-th largest overall. Time: O(nlogk). Space: O(k).

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function findKthLargest(nums,k)heap :=empty min-heap

    for each num in nums
        if size of heap < k
            insert num into heap
        else if num > root of heap
            extract min from heap
            insert num into heap

    return root of heap

```

Time: O(nlogk). Space: O(k).
You'll find this better than sorting when k is small relative to n.

What did you learn?
1. For k-th largest,use a min-heap of size k. For k-th smallest,use a max-heap of size k.
2. The heap root is the "boundary" element. Anything smaller(for k-th largest)gets filtered out.
3. Bounded heaps reduce time complexity from O(nlogn)to O(nlogk). This pattern extends to "top k elements," "k closest points," and similar problems.

Given an integer array nums and an integer k,return the k most frequent elements. Example: nums=[1,1,1,2,2,3],k=2 Output: [1,2](1 appears 3 times,2 appears 2 times)This combines frequency counting(hash maps)with the k-th element pattern(heaps).

questions.title: Top K Frequent Elements - Read Statement

units.text:
Phase 1: Count frequencies using a hash map. Phase 2: Find the k elements with highest frequency using a heap.
For phase 2,you can either:

Use a min-heap of size k(keep k most frequent)Use a max-heap of all elements and extract k times

The min-heap approach is O(nlogk),better when k is small. Time: O(nlogk). Space: O(n).

Here's the solution with a min-heap of size k:
Pseudocode


```
function topKFrequent(nums,k)// Phase 1: Count frequencies
    freq :=empty hash map
    for each num in nums
        freq[num] :=freq[num]+1

    // Phase 2: Find top k by frequency
    heap :=empty min-heap of(frequency,element)pairs

    for each(element,count)in freq
        if size of heap < k
            insert(count,element)into heap
        else if count > frequency at heap root
            extract min from heap
            insert(count,element)into heap

    return all elements from heap

```

Time: O(nlogk). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Top K Frequent Elements - Implement Solution

units.text:
What did you learn?
1. Complex problems often combine data structures. Here: hash map for counting,heap for selection.
2. Heaps can order by any property,not just element value. Here: order by frequency.
3. When using pairs in heaps,the first element determines ordering. Put the comparison key first. This combination(count with map,select with heap)appears in many problems.

1. Initialize a min-heap with the first element from each list(along with list index and position).
2. Extract the minimum. The extracted value is the next element in the merged output.
3. If that list has more elements,insert the next one into the heap.
4. Repeat until heap is empty. The heap always has at most k elements. Each of the n total elements is inserted and extracted once. Total: O(nlogk).

You are given an array of k linked lists,each sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked lists into one sorted linked list.
You're facing a famous interview problem that tests your skills. The heap-based solution is clean and efficient. The constraint is up to 104 lists with up to 500 nodes each.

questions.title: Merge k Sorted Lists - Read Statement

units.text:
Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function mergeKLists(lists)heap :=empty min-heap of(value,listIndex,node)// Initialize with first node from each list
    for i from 0 to length of lists - 1
        if lists[i] is not null
            insert(lists[i].val,i,lists[i])into heap

    dummy :=new ListNode(0)current :=dummy

    while heap is not empty(val,idx,node):=extract min from heap
        current.next :=node
        current :=current.next

        if node.next is not null
            insert(node.next.val,idx,node.next)into heap

    return dummy.next

```

Time: O(nlogk). Space: O(k).

questions.title: Merge k Sorted Lists - Implement Solution

units.text:
What did you learn?
1. Heaps track "frontiers" efficiently. The heap contains one candidate from each source.
2. The heap size is bounded by k,not n. Bounded size gives you O(nlogk)instead of O(nlogn).
3. Store enough metadata in heap entries to know where the next element comes from. This pattern applies to merging any k sorted streams: files,arrays,iterators.

Given a stream of numbers,find the median at any point. The median is the middle element. For odd count,it's the center.
For even count,it's the average of the two center elements. Challenge: after each insertion,you need the median in O(1)time.
Sorting after each insertion is O(nlogn),too slow. A clever technique uses two heaps to maintain O(logn)insertion with O(1)median queries.

The idea: maintain two heaps that partition the elements around the median.
1. Max-heap: contains the smaller half of elements. Its root is the largest small element.
2. Min-heap: contains the larger half of elements. Its root is the smallest large element. The median is:

The root of the larger heap(if sizes differ)Average of both roots(if sizes are equal)Insertion goes into one heap,then you rebalance to keep sizes equal(or differ by 1). Time: O(logn). Space: O(n).

Elements: [1,2,3,4,5]
Max-heap(smaller half): [1,2,3],root=3
Min-heap(larger half): [4,5],root=4
Median: with 5 elements,max-heap is larger. Its root(3)is the median.
If you add 6: Max-heap: [3,2,1],root=3
Min-heap: [4,5,6],root=4
Median: average of roots=(3+4)/2=3.5.

units.unit_name: Problem - Find Median from Data Stream
units.text:
Design a data structure that supports:

 `addNum(num)` : add an integer from the stream
 `findMedian()` : return the median of all elements so far

You're solving the classic two-heaps problem. It tests whether you can maintain the invariants correctly during insertion.

questions.title: Find Median from Data Stream - Read Statement

units.text:
Two invariants to maintain:
1. Size balance: the heaps' sizes differ by at most 1. If they differ,the larger heap has the median.
2. Value ordering: all elements in max-heap(smaller half)are≤all elements in min-heap(larger half). Insertion strategy:

Add to max-heap first(smaller half)Move max-heap's root to min-heap(ensure ordering)If min-heap is larger,move its root back to max-heap(ensure balance)

Here's the solution:
Pseudocode


```
class MedianFinder
    maxHeap :=empty max-heap  // smaller half
    minHeap :=empty min-heap  // larger half

    function addNum(num)insert num into maxHeap
        insert(extract max from maxHeap)into minHeap

        if size of minHeap > size of maxHeap
            insert(extract min from minHeap)into maxHeap

    function findMedian()
if size of maxHeap > size of minHeap
            return root of maxHeap
        return(root of maxHeap+root of minHeap)/ 2.0

```

Time: O(logn)per insertion,O(1)for median. Space: O(n).

questions.title: Find Median from Data Stream - Implement Solution

units.text:
What did you learn?
1. Two heaps can partition data around a median(or any percentile).
2. The insertion order(add to one,transfer,rebalance)ensures both invariants are maintained.
3. This pattern generalizes: use two heaps whenever you need efficient access to a "middle" element. Applications: sliding window median,stream percentiles,game matchmaking(median skill).

Look for these signals:
1. "K-th largest/smallest": bounded heap of size k
2. "Top K elements": heap selection
3. "Merge sorted sequences": k-way merge
4. "Running median or percentile": two heaps
5. "Process by priority": simulation with priority queue
6. "Repeatedly need min/max": basic heap If you're repeatedly extracting extrema,a heap is likely the answer.

Sorting: O(nlogn)to sort,then O(1)to access any element by rank. Heap: O(n)to build(heapify),O(logn)per extraction. Use sorting when:

You need multiple rank queries
You need the entire sorted order
The data doesn't change

Use heaps when:

You only need the top k elements
Data arrives as a stream
You need running extrema

You now have heap proficiency:
1. Heap basics: O(1)peek,O(logn)insert and extract. Stored as arrays.
2. K-th element pattern: use a bounded heap of size k. For k-th largest,use a min-heap.
3. K-way merge: heap tracks the frontier of k candidates. O(nlogk)to merge.
4. Two heaps: partition data around median. O(logn)insertion,O(1)median query.
5. Combining structures: heaps often pair with hash maps(top k frequent)or other structures.
Heaps are your tool when you need efficient,repeated access to extrema. Learn these patterns and a whole class of problems becomes routine. Practice these patterns until they become automatic.

Arrays have a fundamental limitation: inserting or deleting elements in the middle costs O(n)because you must shift everything after the change.
Linked lists solve this problem. Each node stores a value and a pointer to the next node. To insert,you update two pointers. O(1)work regardless of list size.
The tradeoff? You lose random access. Finding the k-th element requires walking k steps from the head. But in many algorithms,you only need sequential access anyway. In this section,I'll teach you pointer manipulation patterns that appear constantly in interviews: reversal,fast-slow pointers for cycle detection,and merging sorted lists.

A singly linked list node contains two fields:  `pseudocode class ListNode:     val: integer     next: ListNode or null`  The  `val`  field stores the data.
The  `next`  field points to the following node,or  `null`  if you've reached the last node. To traverse a list,start at the head and follow  `next`  pointers until you hit  `null` .
The operation takes O(n)time where n is the list length. Memory layout differs from arrays. Array elements sit in contiguous memory,enabling cache-friendly access.
Linked list nodes scatter across memory,causing more cache misses. That's why arrays are faster for sequential access despite both being O(n). Time: O(n). Space: O(1).

Many linked list operations have awkward edge cases when modifying the head. The dummy head technique eliminates them.
Create a dummy node that points to the real head: 
```pseudocode
dummy=new ListNode(0)dummy.next=head
Now


```
You never need special logic for "what if I'm modifying the head?" After your algorithm finishes,return `dummy.next` as the new head. 

The dummy node exists only during processing. You don't include it in the result. This technique is especially useful for deletion operations and merging lists. 

I'll use it throughout this section. Time: $O(n)$. Space: $O(1)$.

```

To insert a new node after a given node  `prev` :  `pseudocode newNode=new ListNode(val)newNode.next=prev.next prev.next=newNode`  Order matters here. If you set  `prev.next=newNode`  first,you lose the reference to the rest of the list.
Inserting at the head is a special case: 
```pseudocode
newNode=new ListNode(val)newNode.next=head
head=newNode
Both


```
Finding that point might take $O(n)$. Time: $O(n)$. Space: $O(1)$.

```

To delete the node after a given node  `prev` : 
```pseudocode
prev.next=prev.next.next
That's


```
But what if you need to delete a node and you only have a reference to that node itself,not its predecessor? You can't easily find the predecessor in a singly linked list. 

The trick: copy the next node's value into the current node,then delete the next node. 
```pseudocode
node.val=node.next.val
node.next=node.next.next

``` This doesn't work for the last node since there's no next node to copy from. Time: $O(n)$. Space: $O(1)$.

```

Given the head of a singly linked list,reverse the list and return the new head. Example: 1→2→3→4→5 becomes 5→4→3→2→1.
You're implementing the most fundamental linked list problem. If you can't reverse a list,you can't pass a coding interview.
The iterative solution requires careful pointer manipulation. The recursive solution reveals how the call stack can replace explicit pointers. Constraints: up to 5000 nodes,values from−5000 to 5000.

The iterative approach uses three pointers:  `prev`,`current`,and  `next` .
Pseudocode


```
prev=null
current=head
while current !=null:
    next=current.next
    current.next=prev
    prev=current
    current=next
return prev

```

Walk through this carefully. At each step:
1. Save  `current.next`  before overwriting it
2. Point  `current.next`  backward to  `prev` 
3. Advance  `prev`  and  `current`  one step
When  `current`  becomes  `null`,`prev`  points to the old tail,which is now the new head.
Time: O(n). Space: O(1).

The recursive approach reverses the rest of the list first,then fixes the current node:
Pseudocode


```
function reverse(head):
    if head==null or head.next==null:
        return head
    newHead=reverse(head.next)head.next.next=head
    head.next=null
    return newHead

```

The insight: after  `reverse(head.next)`  returns,`head.next`  still points to what was the second node. That node is now the tail of the reversed suffix. So  `head.next.next=head`  appends  `head`  to the end.
Time: O(n). Space: O(n)for the call stack.
The iterative version is more space-efficient,but the recursive version is cleaner to write.

You now understand both approaches. The iterative three-pointer technique is more space-efficient. The recursive approach is cleaner to write but uses O(n)stack space.
For interviews,know both. Start with iterative unless the problem particularly asks for recursion.
Common bugs:

Forgetting to save  `next`  before overwriting  `current.next` 
Returning  `current`  instead of  `prev`(current is null at the end)Not handling empty list or single-node list

Test your solution on: empty list,single node,two nodes,longer lists. Time: O(n). Space: O(1).

The fast-slow pointer technique(also called Floyd's algorithm)uses two pointers moving at different speeds.
Pseudocode


```
slow=head
fast=head
while fast !=null and fast.next !=null:
    slow=slow.next
    fast=fast.next.next

```

When  `fast`  reaches the end,`slow`  is at the middle. Why? Fast moves twice as fast,so it covers twice the distance in the same number of steps.
This technique solves three categories of problems:
1. Finding the middle of a list
2. Detecting cycles
3. Finding the start of a cycle
All in O(n)time and O(1)space.

To find the middle node:
Pseudocode


```
slow=head
fast=head
while fast !=null and fast.next !=null:
    slow=slow.next
    fast=fast.next.next
return slow

```

For odd-length lists,`slow`  points to the exact middle. For even-length lists,it points to the second of the two middle nodes.
If you need the first middle node for even-length lists,check  `fast.next !=null and fast.next.next !=null`  instead.
Why is this useful? Many problems require processing the two halves of a list separately. Finding the middle in O(n)time and O(1)space enables this without converting to an array first.

Given the head of a linked list,determine if the list has a cycle. A cycle exists if some node's  `next`  pointer points back to a previous node,creating a loop.
If you traverse naively,you'll loop forever. Example: 1→2→3→4→2(4 points back to 2)has a cycle. The naive approach uses a hash set to track visited nodes.
O(n)space. Can you detect cycles in O(1)space? Constraints: up to 104 nodes.

If there's a cycle,fast and slow pointers will eventually meet inside the cycle.
Pseudocode


```
slow=head
fast=head
while fast !=null and fast.next !=null:
    slow=slow.next
    fast=fast.next.next
    if slow==fast:
        return true
return false

```

Why do they meet? Once both pointers enter the cycle,fast gains one position on slow each step. Since the cycle has finite length,fast will eventually catch up.
If there's no cycle,fast reaches the end and the loop exits normally.
Time: O(n). Space: O(1).
You've implemented Floyd's cycle detection algorithm,one of the most famous algorithms in computer science.

Implement cycle detection using the fast-slow pointer technique. Here's the trick: in a cycle,fast catches up to slow at a rate of one node per iteration.
They must meet within one full cycle. At most n steps.
